assumptions_fr: L’engagement est public, volontaire et suffisamment précis quant à l’objectif de neutralité carbone et à l’horizon visé.
La question porte sur la comptabilisation au titre d’IAS 37 à une date de clôture antérieure ou concomitante aux émissions futures à compenser.
Aucune loi spécifique distincte de l’engagement volontaire n’impose, dans les faits décrits, l’achat de crédits carbone avant que les émissions concernées n’aient eu lieu.

recommendation.justification: Aucune provision n’est comptabilisée pour le seul engagement public ni pour des achats futurs de crédits carbone destinés à compenser des émissions futures. Une provision n’est reconnue qu’à mesure que les émissions à compenser ont effectivement été générées, et seulement si l’engagement a créé une obligation constructive.

approaches.reasoning_fr: Dans cette situation, IAS 37 n’autorise pas une provision au seul moment de l’annonce de neutralité carbone, car il n’existe pas encore d’obligation actuelle résultant d’un événement passé pour les coûts de réduction ou pour l’achat futur de crédits visant des émissions futures (ias37 14, 18, 19, E6). En revanche, si l’engagement public crée une obligation constructive, une provision devient possible uniquement lorsque les émissions que l’entité s’est engagée à compenser ont effectivement été émises; à ce moment, l’achat et la mise au rebut des crédits entraînent une sortie probable de ressources (ias37 10, 14, E6).
conditions_fr: L’engagement public a créé une obligation constructive au sens d’IAS 37.
Les émissions que l’entité s’est engagée à compenser ont déjà été effectivement émises à la date de clôture.
L’obligation n’a pas encore été réglée et son montant peut être estimé de manière fiable.
approaches.practical_implication_fr: Pas de provision initiale pour la promesse de neutralité carbone ni pour des achats futurs de crédits; provision seulement au titre des émissions passées à compenser.

references.excerpt: A provision shall be recognised when: **(a)an entity has a present obligation (legal or constructive) as a result of a past event; (b)it is probable that an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits will be required to settle the obligation; and (c)a reliable estimate can be made of the amount of the obligation. If these conditions are not met, no provision shall be recognised.**

Financial statements deal with the financial position of an entity at the end of its reporting period and not its possible position in the future. Therefore, **no provision is recognised for costs that need to be incurred to operate in the future.** The only liabilities recognised in an entity’s statement of financial position are those that exist at the end of the reporting period.

It is only those obligations arising from past events existing independently of an entity’s future actions (ie the future conduct of its business) that are recognised as provisions. ... **Because the entity can avoid the future expenditure by its future actions... it has no present obligation for that future expenditure and no provision is recognised.**

The following terms are used in this Standard with the meanings specified: ... **A constructive obligation is an obligation that derives from an entity’s actions where: (a)by an established pattern of past practice, published policies or a sufficiently specific current statement, the entity has indicated to other parties that it will accept certain responsibilities; and (b)as a result, the entity has created a valid expectation on the part of those other parties that it will discharge those responsibilities.**

[IFRIC® Update, March 2024, Agenda Decision, ‘ IAS 37 Provisions, Contingent Liabilities and Contingent Assets —Climate-related Commitments’ + ... **the entity does not recognise a provision when it makes the statement in 20X0. At that time, the constructive obligation is not a present obligation as a result of a past event.** ... **the entity does not recognise a provision between 20X0 and 20X9 because it does not have a present obligation as a result of a past event until it has emitted the greenhouse gases it has committed to offset.** ... **as the entity emits greenhouse gases in 20X9 and in subsequent years, it will incur a present obligation to offset these past emissions. If the entity has not yet settled that obligation and a reliable estimate can be made of the amount of the obligation, the entity recognises a provision.** ... **settling the constructive obligation to offset the entity’s remaining annual greenhouse gas emissions will require an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits. The entity will be required to buy and retire carbon credits without receiving any resources embodying economic benefits in exchange.**]

approaches.reasoning_fr: Dans cette situation, si l’engagement public n’a pas encore créé d’obligation actuelle à la date de clôture, ou si l’existence même d’une obligation constructive reste seulement possible, le traitement relève d’un passif éventuel et non d’une provision (ias37 10, 13, 16, 23, E6). Cela vise en pratique la période antérieure aux émissions à compenser, sauf si la possibilité de sortie est éloignée.
conditions_fr: À la date de clôture, l’existence d’une obligation actuelle demeure seulement possible ou plus improbable que probable.
Ou bien une obligation existe mais ne remplit pas les critères de comptabilisation d’une provision.
La possibilité de sortie de ressources n’est pas éloignée.
approaches.practical_implication_fr: Absence de provision; information éventuelle en annexe comme passif éventuel selon l’appréciation des faits à la date de clôture.

references.excerpt: The following terms are used in this Standard with the meanings specified: ... **A contingent liability is: (a)a possible obligation that arises from past events and whose existence will be confirmed only by the occurrence or non‑occurrence of one or more uncertain future events not wholly within the control of the entity; or (b)a present obligation that arises from past events but is not recognised because: (i)it is not probable that an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits will be required to settle the obligation; or (ii)the amount of the obligation cannot be measured with sufficient reliability.**

This Standard distinguishes between: **(a)provisions – which are recognised as liabilities** ... and **(b)contingent liabilities – which are not recognised as liabilities because they are either: (i)possible obligations...; or (ii)present obligations that do not meet the recognition criteria in this Standard**...

On the basis of such evidence: **(a)where it is more likely than not that a present obligation exists at the end of the reporting period, the entity recognises a provision (if the recognition criteria are met); and (b)where it is more likely that no present obligation exists at the end of the reporting period, the entity discloses a contingent liability, unless the possibility of an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits is remote**...

For the purpose of this Standard, **an outflow of resources or other event is regarded as probable if the event is more likely than not to occur**... **Where it is not probable that a present obligation exists, an entity discloses a contingent liability, unless the possibility of an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits is remote**...

[IFRIC® Update, March 2024, Agenda Decision, ‘ IAS 37 Provisions, Contingent Liabilities and Contingent Assets —Climate-related Commitments’ + ... **whether the entity’s statement of its commitments to reduce and offset its greenhouse gas emissions creates a constructive obligation to fulfil those commitments will depend on the facts of the statement and the circumstances surrounding it.** ... **if the statement creates a constructive obligation... the entity does not recognise a provision when it makes the statement in 20X0.**]
